facts.fact: GIN indexes are approximately 3x faster for full-text search lookups than GiST indexes, but GIN indexes take 3x longer to build and are 10x slower to update
GIN indexes are most effective for static data with fewer than 100,000 unique lexemes; GiST indexes handle dynamic data better and are faster for updates but slower for reads
Pre-computing tsvectors in GENERATED ALWAYS STORED columns with GIN indexes eliminates the need to call to_tsvector() on every search, turning full table scans into bitmap index scans
PostgreSQL 12+ allows CTEs to be inlined by default if called only once, removing the optimization fence; however, CTEs called multiple times remain materialized by default unless explicitly inlined
Materialized views can improve dashboard query performance by 350x to 9000x in real-world cases; however, concurrent refreshes require a unique index with no WHERE clause
ts_rank_cd computes relevance based on term frequency, term proximity (cover density), and field priority weights (A=highest, B, C, D); ts_rank only considers term frequency
PostgreSQL's ts_rank and ts_rank_cd do not compute inverse document frequency (IDF), meaning scores do not change as the corpus grows; BM25 accounts for corpus-wide term rarity
Without indexing the join column in recursive CTEs, each iteration performs a sequential scan; a 12ms query can degrade to 400ms on modest datasets without proper indexing
Recursive CTEs with path arrays accumulate memory proportional to path_length × node_count; max_recursive_depth defaults to 1000 as a safety limit
JSONB columns require PostgreSQL to rewrite the entire value to disk even for small modifications; JSONB statistics are not tracked by the query planner, severely impacting plan optimization
Normalized tables with proper indexing outperform denormalized JSONB for filtering on indexed fields; JSONB wins for aggregations when data is already denormalized
A bitmap index scan reads table pages sequentially after fetching row locations from the index, balancing between sequential scans (all pages) and index scans (random access)
PostgreSQL full-text search can replace Elasticsearch for ~80% of use cases without additional infrastructure; Elasticsearch is necessary for the ~20% where search is a core feature
Low-cardinality columns (many rows sharing same value) make indexes ineffective; the planner often chooses sequential scans over index scans for low-cardinality indexes
The MATERIALIZED keyword in PostgreSQL CTEs forces materialization as an optimization fence, preventing the outer query from influencing the inner query's execution plan

examples.title: Blockost's Elasticsearch-to-PostgreSQL Migration
examples.description: Blockost replaced Elasticsearch with PostgreSQL full-text search to eliminate sync complexity between the main database and search index. They found PostgreSQL's native FTS sufficient for their use case and reduced infrastructure overhead.

examples.title: Bilt Rewards – ParadeDB Case Study
examples.description: Bilt Rewards, processing over $36B in rent payments annually (2024), uses PostgreSQL full-text search via ParadeDB extension for search functionality, avoiding separate Elasticsearch infrastructure.

examples.title: Dashboard Query Performance – 9000x Speedup via Materialized Views
examples.description: A Rails-based system achieved 9000x faster dashboard queries by materializing complex aggregations into a view and refreshing every 5 minutes. Query time dropped from ~30s to ~3ms.

examples.title: CTE Optimization Fence – 500x Slowdown in Production
examples.description: Hans Buschmann reported a query that slowed 500x due to CTE materialization preventing row restriction pushdown. Rewriting to inline the CTE restored performance.

examples.title: Recursive CTE with Missing Index – 400ms Regression
examples.description: A simple hierarchical query that should take 12ms degrades to 400ms when the parent_id join column lacks an index. Each recursive iteration becomes a sequential scan.

examples.title: Xata's BM25 Ranking Implementation
examples.description: Xata published a detailed comparison showing that PostgreSQL's ts_rank_cd lacks inverse document frequency, making BM25 extensions necessary for search-primary applications.
examples.metric: BM25 outperforms ts_rank_cd in ranking accuracy

analogies.analogy: A CTE is like a wall between the inner query and the outer query. Without the wall, the outer query's restrictions can reach back and optimize the inner query. With the wall, the inner query executes in full isolation, often wastefully.

A bitmap scan is the Goldilocks zone between sequential and index scans. Instead of jumping randomly through pages (index scan) or reading all pages (sequential scan), it reads the index to find page numbers, then reads those pages sequentially.

GiST indexes are like a sieve that catches most matches but produces some false positives; PostgreSQL then filters those false positives. GIN indexes are more precise, like a mesh screen with no false catches.
Updating a single field in a JSONB column is like editing one word in a printed book: the entire page must be reprinted. In a normalized table, you only change the one cell you need.

Building a path array in a recursive CTE is like adding a car to a train at each iteration. The first iteration has 1 car, the second has 2 cars (duplicated across all rows), the third has 3 cars per row, etc. Memory grows combinatorially.

A materialized view refresh is like a snapshot camera. If your refresh takes 6 minutes but you schedule it every 5 minutes, you have overlapping snapshots (old job still running while new one starts), leading to lock contention.

key_numbers.context: GIN performance degrades significantly beyond this threshold for dynamic data

common_misconceptions.misconception: CTEs always improve query performance by breaking complex queries into readable chunks
common_misconceptions.reality: CTEs act as optimization fences in PostgreSQL < 12, preventing predicate pushdown and causing 10-500x slowdowns. Even in PostgreSQL 12+, multiple-call CTEs remain materialized by default.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Using LIKE or ILIKE is simpler and sufficient for text search
common_misconceptions.reality: LIKE and ILIKE lack index support, require sequential scans of all documents, and are significantly slower than tsvector + GIN indexes. Engineers often overlook PostgreSQL's full built-in text search engine.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Every recursive CTE with path arrays is automatically slow
common_misconceptions.reality: Path arrays are slow because memory grows combinatorially (path_length × node_count), not because of the recursion itself. Limiting depth or materializing to a temp table fixes this.

common_misconceptions.misconception: JSONB is a drop-in replacement for normalized tables with better flexibility
common_misconceptions.reality: JSONB updates require rewriting the entire value to disk, the planner has no statistics on JSONB fields (causing bad query plans), and filtering on indexed normalized columns is consistently faster.

common_misconceptions.misconception: ts_rank is sufficient for relevance ranking in production applications
common_misconceptions.reality: ts_rank and ts_rank_cd lack inverse document frequency, term frequency saturation, and corpus-aware length normalization that BM25 provides. Modern search applications need BM25 extensions.

common_misconceptions.misconception: GIN indexes are always better than GiST for full-text search
common_misconceptions.reality: GIN is 3x faster for reads but 10x slower for updates and 3x slower to build. For dynamic data or frequent writes, GiST may be the better choice despite slower reads.

common_misconceptions.misconception: You must compute tsvector on every search for best results
common_misconceptions.reality: Pre-computing tsvectors in GENERATED ALWAYS STORED columns allows PostgreSQL to index them once and reuse the index for all searches, improving performance dramatically.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Materialized views refresh instantly without overhead
common_misconceptions.reality: Standard refreshes lock the view during refresh (blocking reads). Concurrent refreshes require a unique index and still compete with concurrent queries. Overlapping refresh jobs cause contention.

common_misconceptions.misconception: ts_headline is fast for generating search result snippets
common_misconceptions.reality: ts_headline searches the original document, not the tsvector, making it slow when called for every matching row. Use it sparingly or only for top-N results.

common_misconceptions.misconception: You need Elasticsearch for all production search applications
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL covers ~80% of search use cases without additional infrastructure. Companies like Blockost and Bilt Rewards successfully replaced Elasticsearch with native PostgreSQL FTS.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Low-cardinality columns are fine to index
common_misconceptions.reality: Low-cardinality columns (many rows per value) make indexes ineffective; the planner chooses sequential scans over index scans because the index must be traversed for each matching row.
PostgreSQL has no automatic refresh mechanism. Refreshes must be scheduled via cron jobs, and refresh time must be less than the refresh interval to avoid lock contention.
